mnemonics.title: TAROS Goal
mnemonics.content: Remember the main goals of distributed systems using TAROS: Transparency, Availability, Reliability, Openness, Scalability. Each represents a key design objective in building distributed systems.

mnemonics.title: SCALABLE Dimensions
mnemonics.content: Remember scalability dimensions as SCALABLE: Size, Currency/Geographic, Administrative, Load balancing, Economic, Budget - Wait, think of SGA instead: Size, Geographic, Administrative scaling

mnemonics.title: 6 Types of Transparency
mnemonics.content: Remember transparency types with: ALMACO - Access, Location, Migration, And Concurrency, Replication, Failure transparency

mnemonics.title: Middleware Purpose
mnemonics.content: Middleware = Middle man between apps and network - like a translator helping two people who speak different languages communicate

mnemonics.title: DS Categories
mnemonics.content: Remember 3 categories: CIS (Computing, Information, Pervasive) - like CISCO but S for Systems. Think: 'Computing needs Information to be Pervasive'

acronyms.usage: A collection of independent computers appearing as a single coherent system
Java's mechanism for distributed object communication
Mechanism for programs to call procedures on remote systems
Business application software for integrated business processes
Set of protocols for building software applications, important in middleware design

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a spider web - multiple points (computers) connected, but the web looks like ONE structure. Each point is independent but together they form one coherent pattern.
Think of an iceberg - users only see the tip (the interface), but underneath lies the complex distributed structure hidden from view. What you see is what you get (the unified view).
Think of a restaurant - the client (customer) makes requests, the server (waiter) takes orders to the kitchen (server) and brings back food. One asks, one serves.
Picture a translator at an international conference - bridging the gap between speakers (applications) who speak different languages (use different systems).
Imagine a building with modular floors - you can add more floors (resources) as needed without disrupting the building's function

analogies.analogy: Like a team vs just people in the same room. A computer network is like people in different rooms with phones - they can communicate. A distributed system is like a well-coordinated team with a shared purpose and unified approach.
Like an interpreter at the UN - bridging different languages (heterogeneous systems) so everyone understands each other while appearing seamless to delegates (users).
Like an autopilot in an airplane - passengers don't know (and don't need to know) about the complex systems working; they just experience a smooth flight.
Like a backup singer taking over seamlessly when the lead singer forgets lyrics - the audience (user) doesn't notice anything happened.
